All VIFs fall below 5 after removing death. The bars are now uniformly green — the model is structurally sound.
Crucially, the R² barely changes from model_full to model1. This confirms that death was not adding new information — it was merely duplicating what positive and recovered already encode.






6.3 Checking Normality of Residuals
check_normality() overlays the empirical residual distribution against the theoretical normal curve, and produces a Q-Q plot for tail behaviour.


Show code
check_n <- check_normality(model1)
plot(check_n)















NoteWhat I Learned




Residuals are approximately normal in the centre but show mild heavy tails — common for rate outcomes bounded at zero (CFR cannot go negative). This level of deviation is acceptable at our sample size (n ≈ 260) via the Central Limit Theorem.
If the tails were severely non-normal, the next step would be a Box-Cox transformation of CFR — but that is not needed here.






6.4 Checking Homogeneity of Variances
check_heteroscedasticity() plots √|standardised residuals| against fitted values. A flat, horizontal band indicates constant variance.


Show code
check_h <- check_heteroscedasticity(model1)
plot(check_h)















NoteWhat I Learned




The reference line is broadly flat — no strong funnel pattern, so homoscedasticity holds reasonably well.
Slight spread at the extremes corresponds to a handful of sub-districts with very high CFR. These are real-world anomalies, not data errors — retaining them is appropriate.






6.5 Complete Diagnostic Check — check_model()
check_model() consolidates all individual diagnostics into a single six-panel dashboard. This is the most efficient way to audit all linear regression assumptions simultaneously.


Show code
check_model(model1)















NoteWhat I Learned



The six panels address six distinct questions — each one either clears a concern or flags it:








Panel
Question
Finding




Posterior predictive check
Does the model reproduce the observed distribution?
Good overlap — model captures the bulk of CFR values


Linearity (Residuals vs Fitted)
Is the outcome-predictor relationship truly linear?
Reference line flat → linearity holds


Homoscedasticity
Is residual spread constant across fitted values?
Broadly yes; minor spread at extremes


Influential observations (Cook’s D)
Do any sub-districts distort the whole model?
A few points exceed threshold — but represent real variation


Collinearity (VIF)
Are any predictors redundant?
All green after removing death


Normality of residuals (Q-Q)
Are residuals Gaussian?
Acceptable; slight heavy tails



The influential observations panel is the one most often overlooked. High Cook’s Distance means a single sub-district, if removed, would shift coefficients noticeably. Here those points are genuine high-CFR cases — not data errors — so we retain them and note their influence in interpretation.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d
text:
7 Visualising Regression Parameters
With all diagnostics cleared, we can now interpret the model coefficients with confidence. Two complementary methods are demonstrated.

7.1 Method 1: plot(parameters::parameters()) — see Package
parameters() from the easystats ecosystem extracts model parameters; plot() from see renders them as a dot-and-whisker coefficient plot. The explicit namespace parameters::parameters() is mandatory here because tidymodels loads dials, which exports its own parameters() for hyperparameter tuning.


Show code
plot(parameters::parameters(model1), show_intercept = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title    = "Regression Coefficients — CFR Model (DKI Jakarta)",
    subtitle = "Point estimates with 95% confidence intervals"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)















NoteWhat I Learned




recovered has the largest negative coefficient — sub-districts where more cases resolve into recoveries have systematically lower CFR. This is the single most important driver in the model and is analytically coherent: recovery dynamics reflect healthcare capacity.
City effects are statistically significant and substantial — even after conditioning on case volume and recovery, geography explains residual CFR differences. This points to unmeasured city-level factors: healthcare infrastructure, testing rates, demographic composition.
positive sits near zero — once recovery and active case load are in the model, total confirmed cases carry minimal marginal signal. Volume alone does not predict fatality.
A coefficient whose CI does not cross zero is statistically distinguishable from no effect. The see plot makes this visible at a glance without reading a table.






7.2 Method 2: ggcoefstats() — ggstatsplot Package
ggcoefstats() embeds coefficient estimates, t-statistics, and p-value annotations directly on the plot — making it self-contained for reports and presentations.


Show code
ggcoefstats(
  x            = model1,
  output       = "plot",
  title        = "Regression Coefficients — CFR Model (DKI Jakarta)",
  subtitle     = "Unstandardised estimates with 95% CIs and p-value annotations",
  point.shape  = 15,
  point.color  = "white",
  point.fill   = "#e74c3c",
  stats.labels = TRUE
) +
  theme(
    axis.text.y = element_text(margin = margin(t = 4, b = 4)),
    plot.margin = margin(t = 10, b = 10)
  )















NoteWhat I Learned




ggcoefstats() shows unstandardised (raw) coefficients in the original unit (% CFR), while plot(parameters::parameters()) shows standardised ones. Both are useful — standardised for comparing relative importance across predictors, unstandardised for direct real-world interpretation.
The embedded p-value labels and estimate annotations make the plot stand alone without a companion table — practical for policy-facing reports where readers cannot look up a regression table.
Together, both methods confirm the same story: recovery rate and city are the dominant structural drivers of CFR. Raw case counts, once composition is controlled for, fade into statistical irrelevance.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d
text:
8 Summary
The full workflow — from variable screening to parameter communication — follows a single logical thread:



Visualising Models — Step-by-Step Summary


#
Step
Key function
What it revealed




1
Variable screening
SmartEDA::ExpCatStat()
death_bin has the highest Cramér's V — circularity risk before any model is fit.


2
Initial model
lm(cfr ~ positive + recovered + death + active + city_label)
R² looks fine; estimates are not. Surface fit masks structural instability.


3
Multicollinearity check
performance::check_collinearity() · plot()
death VIF >> 10. Removal required before any coefficient can be trusted.


4
Reduced model
lm(cfr ~ positive + recovered + active + city_label)
All VIF < 5. Model is structurally sound. R² barely changes — death added no real information.


5
Normality of residuals
performance::check_normality() · plot()
Residuals approximately normal; mild heavy tails acceptable at n ≈ 260.


6
Homoscedasticity check
performance::check_heteroscedasticity() · plot()
Variance broadly constant; minor spread at extremes from genuine high-CFR outliers.


7
Complete diagnostic
performance::check_model()
All six panels pass. A few influential sub-districts noted but retained as real variation.


8
Coefficient plot (see)
plot(parameters::parameters(model1))
recovered has the largest significant negative effect; city effects persist after case adjustment.


9
Coefficient plot (ggcoefstats)
ggstatsplot::ggcoefstats(model1, output = 'plot')
Same story with p-values and raw estimates embedded — ready for non-technical audiences.





The story in one paragraph: The ExpCatStat() EDA signalled before any model was fit that death would create circularity — CFR is constructed from it. The initial model confirmed this: check_collinearity() showed extreme VIFs. Dropping death and refitting gave a structurally clean model that passed all three diagnostic checks. Visualising the parameters then revealed the substantive finding: across Jakarta sub-districts, where a patient is located (city) and whether they recover (recovered) matter far more for fatality than how many people were infected (positive). Visualising models is not just validation — it is the mechanism by which data tells its story, and by which a decision-maker can trust the answer.

title: In-class Exercise 3
text:
NoteOverview



This exercise documents two Tableau dashboards built during class, covering:

Part 1 — Superstore dataset: quadrant analysis, Pareto charts, and annual trend analysis.
Part 2 — Singapore population dataset (respopagesextod2025): population pyramids and trellis small multiples by Planning Area.

Each section embeds the live Tableau dashboard, explains how the data was prepared and published, and summarises the key analytical insights derived from class.

title: In-class Exercise 3
text: 1.1 Tableau Story

title: In-class Exercise 3
section: 1.2 Data Preparation & Publishing to Tableau Public
text:
1.2 Data Preparation & Publishing to Tableau Public
The Superstore dataset is an Excel workbook (Superstore.xls) with three sheets: Orders, Returns, and People. The Orders sheet is the primary data source containing all transactional records.
Step-by-step workflow in class:

Connect the data — Open Tableau Desktop → Connect → Microsoft Excel → select Superstore.xls. Tableau auto-detects field types: dates, text dimensions, and numeric measures are ready to use without manual transformation.
Create calculated fields — The following computed measures were added in the formula editor to power the Pareto and quadrant analyses:

Profit Ratio → SUM([Profit]) / SUM([Sales])
Running Sum of Profit → RUNNING_SUM(SUM([Profit]))
Cumulative Profit % → RUNNING_SUM(SUM([Profit])) / TOTAL(SUM([Profit]))
Running Count of Customers → RUNNING_COUNT(COUNTD([Customer ID]))

Build each worksheet — One worksheet per Story Point was built, then stitched together into a Tableau Story (works like a presentation — each Story Point = one slide).
Publish to Tableau Public — Server → Tableau Public → Save to Tableau Public As… uploads the workbook and generates a shareable URL and embed <iframe> code instantly.







Tip



Embed in Quarto: From any published Tableau Public viz, click the Share icon in the bottom toolbar → copy the <iframe> embed code → paste it inside a ```{=html} block in the Quarto .qmd file.

title: In-class Exercise 3
section: 1.3 What Was Learned — Story Point by Story Point
text:
1.3 What Was Learned — Story Point by Story Point

1.3.1 Story Point 1: Sales vs Profit Quadrant by State
Every US state is placed on a scatter plot using percentile of total sales on the X-axis and percentile of total profit on the Y-axis. Two reference lines at the 50th percentile divide the canvas into four labelled quadrants. Bubble size encodes total sales; colour encodes actual profit (orange = loss, blue = gain).






Note



Technique: Quadrant Analysis using Percentile Ranks
Using percentile ranks instead of raw dollar values is the critical design decision. Raw values would collapse most states into the lower-left corner because a handful of large states (California, New York, Texas) dominate the scale. Percentile ranks spread all 49 points evenly across the full canvas, making every state visible and comparable.
The four quadrants map directly to business decisions:









Quadrant
Label
Interpretation
Recommended Action




Top-right
⭐ Stars
High Sales & High Profit
Protect, invest, replicate


Bottom-right
⚠️ Value Destroyers
High Sales but Low Profit
Diagnose discounting / product mix


Top-left
🔷 Hidden Gems
Low Sales but profitable
Expand marketing presence


Bottom-left
💤 Laggards
Low Sales & Low Profit
Deprioritise resources





Key Insight: States in the bottom-right quadrant are the most strategically dangerous — they consume significant resources (sales effort, logistics, inventory) without delivering proportionate profit. Texas and Pennsylvania, for example, generate high revenue but poor returns. The diagnosis in most cases traces back to aggressive discounting in those markets, which the quadrant view alone doesn’t show but prompts the analyst to investigate next.



1.3.2 Story Point 2: Pareto Chart by Customer ID
Every customer is ranked from highest to lowest total profit and plotted on a cumulative curve. The Y-axis shows the running percentage of total profit accumulated; the X-axis shows the percentage of the customer base reached so far. A reference line at 80% reveals the Pareto threshold.






Note



Technique: Pareto / 80-20 Cumulative Profit Curve
The 80/20 rule (Pareto Principle) states that roughly 80% of outcomes come from 20% of inputs. In Tableau, the cumulative curve is built using:

RUNNING_SUM(SUM([Profit])) — the running total of profit as customers are added in descending order.
Dividing by TOTAL(SUM([Profit])) — converts to a cumulative percentage.
A reference line at Y = 80% — shows exactly how many customers (as % of the base) are needed to reach 80% of total profit.

The curve shape tells the story: a steep early rise means profit is highly concentrated in a small customer elite. A gradual, uniform rise means profit is spread evenly and no customer is disproportionately important.


Key Insight: The curve rises very steeply in the early section — meaning a small minority of customers, roughly the top 20%, generate approximately 80% of cumulative profit. This finding directly informs CRM strategy: loyalty programmes, dedicated account managers, and retention initiatives should be directed intensively at that top-tier group. Indiscriminate marketing to the full customer base wastes budget on the long tail that contributes little to profit.



1.3.3 Story Point 3: Annual Sales and Profit
Total annual sales are displayed as a bar chart and total annual profit is overlaid as a line with markers — a dual-axis view that allows simultaneous reading of both revenue volume and profit quality.






Note



Technique: Dual-Axis Chart (Bar + Line)
In Tableau, dual-axis charts are built by: 1. Placing two measures on the Rows shelf. 2. Right-clicking the second pill → Dual Axis → Synchronise Axis (if scales are similar) or leaving them independent (if one measure is much larger than the other). 3. Assigning different mark types to each axis — Bar for Sales (volume), Line+Circle for Profit (quality).
The visual separation of sales (bars = magnitude) from profit (line = trend direction) in a single view prevents the common mistake of conflating revenue growth with profit health.


Key Insight: Sales revenue grows consistently year-on-year, but profit growth is uneven. In some years profit climbs proportionally with sales; in others it stagnates or dips while sales continue to grow — a warning sign that discounting pressure or product mix deterioration is eroding margins. Tracking the profit margin percentage alongside absolute profit prevents leadership from celebrating a sales record that masks underlying value destruction.



1.3.4 Story Point 4: Pareto Chart by Sub-Category
The Pareto logic is applied to sub-categories instead of customers. A bar chart sorted in descending profit order is combined with a cumulative percentage line on a secondary Y-axis, and a horizontal reference line marks the 80% profit threshold.






Note



Technique: Pareto Bar Chart with Dual Y-axis and Reference Line
This is the classic Pareto chart format:

Left Y-axis → absolute total profit per sub-category (descending bars).
Right Y-axis → cumulative % of total profit (line, 0–100%).
80% dashed reference line → the “vital few / trivial many” boundary.

Sub-categories to the left of the 80% line are the vital few that must be protected and grown. Sub-categories to the right are candidates for repricing, discounting reform, or discontinuation.
Critically, loss-making sub-categories (negative bars, shown in red) drag the cumulative line downward — their removal would actually increase the 80% threshold value, improving overall profitability even at the cost of some revenue.


Key Insight: Copiers, Phones, and Accessories are the profit powerhouses — a small set that drives the vast majority of positive profit. Tables and Bookcases, on the other hand, generate net losses. The Pareto chart makes this concentration visually unambiguous in a way that a plain table of numbers does not. The recommended action is clear: double down on the vital few, and implement hard discount caps or price corrections for the loss-makers.

title: In-class Exercise 3
section: 2.2 Data Preparation & Publishing to Tableau Public
text:
2.2 Data Preparation & Publishing to Tableau Public
The respopagesextod2025 dataset is published by the Singapore Department of Statistics (SingStat) and records Singapore’s resident population by Planning Area, Subzone, five-year Age Group, Sex, and Type of Dwelling, as at June 2025.
Step-by-step workflow:

Download the dataset — Visit www.singstat.gov.sg → Find Data → Population → Geographic Distribution → Latest Data → download respopagesextod2025.csv. The file has six columns: PA, SZ, AG, Sex, TOD, Pop.
Inspect and understand the structure — Each row represents one combination of (Planning Area × Subzone × Age Group × Sex × Type of Dwelling) with its population count. Many rows have Pop = 0 (no residents for that combination). The AG column uses coded labels such as 0_to_4, 5_to_9, … 85_and_over.
Connect in Tableau Desktop — Connect → Text File → select the CSV. Ensure Pop is treated as a Measure (green pill) and PA, SZ, AG, Sex, TOD are all Dimensions (blue pills). Filter Pop > 0 using a data source filter to exclude empty rows before any chart is built.
Create the pyramid calculated field — A new field is needed to mirror male values to the left side of the chart:

Field name: Population (Pyramid)
Formula: IF [Sex] = "Males" THEN -[Pop] ELSE [Pop] END

Sort the Age Group axis correctly — By default, Tableau sorts AG alphabetically, which produces a nonsensical order (0_to_4 → 10_to_14 → 15_to_19…). Right-click the AG pill → Sort → Manual → drag the values into the correct ascending age order from 0–4 at the bottom to 85+ at the top.
Build the trellis — With AG on Rows and Population (Pyramid) on Columns, drag PA onto the Columns shelf above the measure. Tableau automatically repeats the pyramid for every Planning Area, creating the small-multiples layout.
Publish to Tableau Public — Server → Tableau Public → Save to Tableau Public As… and copy the embed code.







Tip



Sorting tip: The 5-year age group codes in SingStat files sort alphabetically in a wrong order. Always set a manual sort in Tableau to arrange them correctly before adding any other dimensions. Getting the age axis right is the most important step for a readable pyramid.

title: In-class Exercise 3
section: 2.3 What Was Learned — Population Analysis Techniques
text:
2.3 What Was Learned — Population Analysis Techniques

2.3.1 The Population Pyramid
The population pyramid is the foundational demographic chart, encoding three variables simultaneously: age group (Y-axis, youngest at bottom), sex (left = males, right = females), and population count (bar length, mirrored horizontally). The resulting silhouette immediately communicates the overall age structure.






Note



Technique: Mirrored Horizontal Bar Chart
Three classic shapes each carry a distinct demographic message:








Shape
Name
What it tells us




Wide base tapering to top
Expansive
High birth rate — young, fast-growing population


Narrow base, wide middle
Constrictive
Low birth rate — ageing population


Nearly uniform width
Stationary
Low growth — stable, replacement-level fertility



Singapore’s national pyramid is clearly constrictive: the 0–14 base is markedly narrower than the 25–54 working-age bulge. This is the demographic signature of low fertility combined with high life expectancy — a trend that is accelerating as the large Baby Boomer cohort enters their 70s and 80s.


Key Insight: The growing 65+ elderly cohort will place increasing pressure on healthcare infrastructure, CPF systems, and social services over the next two decades. The shrinking youth base means fewer workers entering the labour force to support that growing elderly population. Spatial awareness of where the elderly are concentrated is essential for targeted infrastructure investment — which is precisely why the trellis view matters.



2.3.2 Trellis Population Pyramids by Planning Area
The headline technique from this class session is the trellis (small multiples) layout — one mini population pyramid per Planning Area, all shown side by side on a single canvas for direct visual comparison.






Note



Technique: Small Multiples / Trellis Chart
Small multiples follow Edward Tufte’s foundational principle: show the same chart type repeated across different categories so that comparison is immediate and cognitive load is minimised. A viewer does not need to switch between tabs or remember what a previous chart looked like — all information is spatially co-located.
Reading the trellis across Singapore’s planning areas reveals three recurring demographic archetypes:









Archetype
Pyramid Shape
Characteristics
Example Areas




Young Town
Wide base
Many children, few elderly — families moved in recently
Punggol, Sengkang


Mature Estate
Barrel-shaped
Even age distribution across most groups
Tampines, Jurong West


Ageing Estate
Top-heavy
Narrow base, large 55–75 band — residents ageing in place
Bukit Timah, Marine Parade



The trellis makes these archetypes visually obvious in a way that no table or individual chart could achieve.


Key Insights:

New towns built after 2000 (Punggol, Sengkang) show dramatically wide bases — young families who moved in together are now raising children, producing a characteristic bottom-heavy pyramid.
Established estates from the 1970s–80s (Marine Parade, Toa Payoh) have inverted profiles — the original residents are still living there and have aged together, creating top-heavy shapes and high old-age dependency.
Type of Dwelling adds a further layer: when TOD is used as a filter or colour, HDB 3–5 Room flats dominate most planning areas numerically, while private condominiums show a distinctly different profile skewed toward the 30–50 age band — reflecting the typical first-time private buyer demographic.

title: In-class Exercise 3
text:
2.4 Summary of Key Learnings






NoteClass Takeaways



Superstore — analytical patterns learned:

Quadrant analysis is more readable than raw scatter plots for multi-entity comparison because percentile ranking distributes every point evenly across the canvas regardless of scale differences. The business action implied by each quadrant is immediately obvious.
Pareto charts are the go-to for concentration analysis. The 80/20 rule appears consistently — in customers, in sub-categories, and in geographic markets. Identifying the vital few is always the first step before making resource allocation decisions.
Dual-axis trend charts prevent the common management trap of celebrating revenue growth while margins quietly deteriorate. Showing sales and profit together in one view forces the question: are we growing in a healthy way?

Population data — analytical patterns learned:

Population pyramids pack three variables (age, sex, count) into one compact, highly readable chart. Learning to interpret the shape — expansive, constrictive, or stationary — is a transferable skill applicable to any demographic dataset globally.
Trellis / small multiples is the correct pattern whenever you need to compare the same chart type across many geographic or categorical units simultaneously. It preserves spatial context while enabling immediate pattern recognition across panels.
Derived metrics (old-age dependency ratio, youth dependency ratio) transform raw population counts into planning-relevant indicators that are meaningful to policy makers and non-technical stakeholders.

Tableau workflow habits established in class:

Filter out zero-population rows at the data source level before building any chart.
Always manually sort age group dimensions — alphabetical sorting produces a nonsensical axis for coded age labels.
Use percentile rank table calculations to normalise axes in scatter plots involving entities at very different scales.
Publish to Tableau Public early and often — the embed process into Quarto takes under two minutes once the workbook is online.

3.3 The Discount Problem

# Analyze relationship between discount and profitability
discount_analysis <- superstore %>%
  mutate(
    Profit_Status = ifelse(Profit > 0, "Profitable", "Unprofitable"),
    Discount_Range = cut(Discount, 
                        breaks = c(0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0),
                        labels = c("0-10%", "11-20%", "21-30%", "31-50%", "50%+"),
                        include.lowest = TRUE)
  )

# Compare average discount
discount_comparison <- superstore %>%
  mutate(Profit_Status = ifelse(Profit > 0, "Profitable", "Unprofitable")) %>%
  group_by(Profit_Status) %>%
  summarise(
    Avg_Discount = mean(Discount),
    Count = n(),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit)
  )

3.5 Temporal Analysis

# Prepare data for time series analysis
monthly_trends <- superstore %>%
  mutate(
    Year = year(`Order Date`),
    Month = floor_date(`Order Date`, "month")

    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),

  mutate(Month = floor_date(`Order Date`, "month")) %>%
  group_by(Month) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales = sum(Sales),

    Profit_Margin = (Total_Profit / Total_Sales) * 100

p6 <- ggplot(monthly_overall, aes(x = Month, y = Profit_Margin)) +
  geom_line(color = "#2c3e50", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = TRUE, color = "#e74c3c", alpha = 0.2) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(scale = 1)) +
  labs(
    title = "Monthly Profit Margin Trend",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Profit Margin (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Analyze by customer segment
segment_analysis <- superstore %>%
  group_by(Segment) %>%
  summarise(
    Total_Sales = sum(Sales),
    Total_Profit = sum(Profit),
    Avg_Profit = mean(Profit),
    Count = n(),
    Avg_Discount = mean(Discount)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Total_Profit))

4.1 Summary of Critical Findings

# Create summary table of key metrics
key_metrics <- tibble(
  Metric = c(
    "Total Transactions",
    "Total Sales",
    "Total Profit",
    "Overall Profit Margin",
    "Unprofitable Transactions",
    "Loss from Unprofitable Transactions",
    "Most Profitable Category",
    "Least Profitable Sub-Category"
  ),
  Value = c(
    format(nrow(superstore), big.mark = ","),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Sales)),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Profit)),
    paste0(round((sum(superstore$Profit) / sum(superstore$Sales)) * 100, 2), "%"),
    paste0(format(sum(superstore$Profit < 0), big.mark = ","), 
           " (", round(mean(superstore$Profit < 0) * 100, 1), "%)"),
    dollar(sum(superstore$Profit[superstore$Profit < 0])),
    "Technology",
    "Tables"
  )
)
